items.question_text: Why should a proposed change be submitted well before the cutoff date for completion?
items.explanation: Per 1-1-6, proposed changes must be submitted well in advance because review and coordination often take several months before the change can be fully coordinated and signed.

choices.text: Because review and coordination often take several months before the change can be fully coordinated and signed.
Because the cutoff date is only the first day local facilities may begin discussing the proposal.
Because publication timing depends only on when local software is installed.
Because an unsigned proposal may be submitted as late as the effective date.

items.question_text: What can justify a waiver to this order?
items.explanation: Per 1-1-11, waivers are justified by exceptional or unusual requirements that require procedural deviations or supplementary procedures.

choices.text: Exceptional or unusual requirements that call for procedural deviations or supplementary procedures.
A local preference for using a simpler version of the standard procedure.
Routine staffing shortages during a normal shift.
Any temporary desire to avoid complying with the order while training is underway.

items.question_text: Which statement best reflects the role of safety in the ATO Safety Management System?
items.explanation: Per 1-1-12, safety is fundamental to air traffic management and communication, navigation, and surveillance services, and all ATO employees share responsibility for owning and executing SMS.

choices.text: Safety is fundamental, and all employees at all levels of the ATO share responsibility for owning and executing SMS.
Safety belongs primarily to the safety office, while operational personnel focus only on efficiency.
Safety becomes part of SMS only after a reportable event has already occurred.
Safety is a management-only responsibility unless a controller is assigned as an SMS specialist.

items.question_text: When this order changes, what is each organization responsible for doing?
items.explanation: Per 1-1-14, organizations are responsible for viewing, downloading, and subscribing to receive email notifications when changes occur to the order.

choices.text: Viewing, downloading, and subscribing to receive FAA email notifications about the changes.
Waiting for a printed replacement copy to arrive by mail before taking any action.
Relying on adjacent facilities to mention changes verbally when convenient.
Checking local supplements only, because electronic distribution is optional.

items.question_text: What is a note used for in this order?
items.explanation: Per 1-2-3, notes present statements of fact or prefatory or explanatory material related to directive text.

choices.text: To present statements of fact or prefatory or explanatory material related to directive text.
To create a new mandatory requirement that overrides directive text.
To identify military-only procedures that do not apply to civil operations.
To replace references to supporting publications or figures.

items.question_text: When should an established timed-approach sequence be interrupted?
items.explanation: Per 6-7-3, interrupt the timed-approach sequence if necessary to allow an aircraft to execute a different type of approach.

choices.text: When necessary to allow an aircraft to execute a different type of approach.
Whenever the next aircraft reports the outer marker inbound.
Whenever radar contact is established with the following aircraft.
Whenever the tower requests a time check for convenience.

items.question_text: When must you issue a time check before specifying a time to leave the approach fix inbound?
items.explanation: Per 6-7-6, a time check is issued before specifying the time to leave the approach fix inbound unless the aircraft is vectored to the final approach course.

choices.text: Before specifying the time, unless the aircraft is vectored to the final approach course.
Only after the aircraft reports leaving the approach fix inbound.
Only when the aircraft is established on final and not holding.
Never, because timed approaches assume the aircraft already has an accurate clock.

items.question_text: Complete the prescribed phraseology: "RESUME _____ VFR ALTITUDES"
items.explanation: Per 7-8-5, the prescribed phraseology is "RESUME APPROPRIATE VFR ALTITUDES."

choices.text: APPROPRIATE

items.question_text: Why must automation entries be made in EDST without delay?
items.explanation: Per 13-1-10, conflict-probe accuracy depends on current trajectory data, so stale entries can mislead both the sector and surrounding facilities.

choices.text: Because stale trajectory-model data can make aircraft entries and conflict-probe notifications misleading for both the sector and surrounding facilities.
Because timely entries matter only for keeping the aircraft list visually organized.
Because forecast wind updates are the only data source conflict probe actually uses.
Because interim altitude updates are always optional unless the next sector specifically asks for them.

items.question_text: How may delay information be recorded in EDST operations?
items.explanation: Per 13-1-11, delay information must be recorded and may be captured automatically through ERAM hold tools or manually through approved facility methods.

choices.text: Automatically through ERAM hold tools or manually on flight progress strips or facility-approved worksheets, in accordance with the facility standard.
Only manually on flight progress strips, because ERAM hold tools do not satisfy the requirement.
Only after the flight exits holding, regardless of whether any approved tool captured the delay.
Only when TMU specifically requests the information.

items.question_text: When using ERAM Hold Data Menu or Hold View, when is the delay recorded automatically?
items.explanation: Per 13-1-11, with Hold Data Menu or Hold View the delay is automatically recorded when the aircraft is cleared out of hold.

choices.text: When the aircraft is cleared out of hold.
As soon as the aircraft enters holding.
Only when the aircraft checks in with the next sector.
Only after a supervisor manually confirms the hold duration.

items.question_text: What is the ESDT overdue-aircraft notification based on?
items.explanation: Per 13-1-12, ESDT overdue-aircraft notification is based on radar track data.

choices.text: Radar track data.
Only the aircraft's filed route and estimated times over fixes.
Voice position reports received from the pilot on frequency.
The most recent manual entry in the aircraft-list remarks field.

items.question_text: If current forecast wind data are unavailable, how should conflict probe and trial planning be handled?
items.explanation: Per 13-1-14, continue using conflict probe and trial planning, but recognize that alert and trajectory data may be affected.

choices.text: Continue using them, recognizing that alert and trajectory data may be affected.
Stop using both tools completely until forecast wind data are restored.
Use conflict probe only, because trial planning becomes prohibited without forecast winds.
Rely on the alert output without qualification, because missing forecast winds affect only display cosmetics.

items.question_text: If connectivity to an adjacent ERAM facility is lost, how should EDST still be used?
items.explanation: Per 13-1-15, EDST remains in use, but the team must recognize that alert data may be affected.

choices.text: Continue using EDST, but recognize that alert data may be affected.
Stop using EDST until connectivity is fully restored, because its alert logic becomes invalid.
Continue using EDST with no qualification, because adjacent-facility connectivity does not affect alerting.
Use only the aircraft list and suspend all strategic-planning tools immediately.

items.question_text: During a surveillance or flight-data outage, how may electronic flight data still be used?
items.explanation: Per 13-1-16, electronic flight data may support situational awareness while the facility transitions to alternate automation capabilities or nonradar procedures.

choices.text: To support situational awareness while the facility transitions to alternate automation capabilities or nonradar procedures.
As a full replacement for radar separation until all surveillance returns.
Only as a historical archive, not for any operational awareness during the transition.
Only after the facility has fully transitioned to nonradar procedures.

items.question_text: Which item is one of the ERAM Airspace Configuration Elements?
items.explanation: Per 13-1-17, the Airspace Configuration Elements are Special Activity Airspace, Airport Stream Filters, and adapted restrictions.

choices.text: Airport Stream Filters (ASF).
Embedded Route Text (ERT).
The aircraft-list remarks indicator.
A controller-applied Stop Probe entry.

items.question_text: Unless an LOA or facility directive says otherwise, what does activating or scheduling Special Activity Airspace in the Airspace Status View not do by itself?
items.explanation: Per 13-1-17, activating or scheduling SAA in the Airspace Status View does not by itself constitute coordination for activation of the airspace unless an LOA or facility directive provides otherwise.

choices.text: It does not by itself constitute coordination for activation of the airspace.
It does not display the SAA status to other sectors.
It does not allow the sector team to update the element's status.
It does not apply when the SAA is scheduled in advance.

items.question_text: If your sector is assigned responsibility for Airspace Configuration Elements, what must the sector team do when an element's status is modified?
items.explanation: Per 13-1-17, an assigned sector team updates the elements to current status and notifies the operations supervisor when status is modified.

choices.text: Keep the element current and notify the operations supervisor when the status is modified.
Treat the display update itself as the only required action.
Wait for the next sector to confirm the change before updating anything locally.
Update only Special Activity Airspace items, because Airport Stream Filters and adapted restrictions are not configuration elements.
